dependencies.description: Requires physical layer connectivity — copper or fiber with proper speed/duplex negotiation
dependencies.name: Physical Ethernet connectivity

failure_modes.description: Two ends of an 802.1Q trunk have different native VLANs configured. Untagged traffic from one side is placed into the wrong VLAN on the other, causing traffic leaking between VLANs (security risk) and connectivity loss for devices on either native VLAN. Tagged VLAN traffic works normally, making this hard to detect.

resolutions.action: Check native VLAN on both ends: 'show interfaces trunk' — native VLAN column must match
Check native VLAN on both ends: 'show interfaces trunk' — native VLAN column must match
Check native VLAN on both ends: 'show interfaces trunk' — native VLAN column must match
Set matching native VLAN: 'switchport trunk native vlan <N>' on both ends
Set matching native VLAN: 'switchport trunk native vlan <N>' on both ends
Set matching native VLAN: 'switchport trunk native vlan <N>' on both ends
Best practice: use an unused VLAN as native (e.g., VLAN 999) and tag all real traffic
Best practice: use an unused VLAN as native (e.g., VLAN 999) and tag all real traffic
Best practice: use an unused VLAN as native (e.g., VLAN 999) and tag all real traffic
Enable 'vlan dot1q tag native' (IOS-XE) to tag the native VLAN and eliminate the ambiguity entirely
Enable 'vlan dot1q tag native' (IOS-XE) to tag the native VLAN and eliminate the ambiguity entirely
Enable 'vlan dot1q tag native' (IOS-XE) to tag the native VLAN and eliminate the ambiguity entirely

root_causes.cause: Different 'switchport trunk native vlan <N>' configured on each end of the trunk
One end configured as access port (native VLAN = access VLAN) and the other as trunk
Default native VLAN (1) on one end, changed native VLAN on the other
Copy-paste error during switch provisioning

failure_modes.scenario: Native VLAN mismatch on trunk link
symptoms: CDP/LLDP syslog: '%CDP-4-NATIVE_VLAN_MISMATCH: Native VLAN mismatch discovered on <intf>'
Hosts on the native VLAN have intermittent or no connectivity
Traffic from VLAN X on one switch appears in VLAN Y on the other
Security concern: untagged traffic crosses VLAN boundaries
All tagged VLANs work fine — only untagged (native VLAN) traffic is affected

failure_modes.description: One side of a link is hard-coded to full duplex while the other uses auto-negotiate. The auto-negotiate side falls back to half duplex per IEEE spec (cannot detect the peer's duplex when the peer doesn't negotiate). The link comes up and appears functional, but performance is severely degraded with late collisions, FCS errors, and runt frames. Often misdiagnosed as a 'slow network' problem.

resolutions.action: Check both ends: 'show interface status' — verify speed and duplex match
Check both ends: 'show interface status' — verify speed and duplex match
Check both ends: 'show interface status' — verify speed and duplex match
Best practice: use auto-negotiate on both ends (IEEE recommendation). If one end must be forced, force BOTH ends to the same speed and duplex
Best practice: use auto-negotiate on both ends (IEEE recommendation). If one end must be forced, force BOTH ends to the same speed and duplex
Best practice: use auto-negotiate on both ends (IEEE recommendation). If one end must be forced, force BOTH ends to the same speed and duplex
For legacy devices that don't support auto-negotiate: force both ends to match
For legacy devices that don't support auto-negotiate: force both ends to match
For legacy devices that don't support auto-negotiate: force both ends to match
Clear interface counters after fixing to verify errors stop: 'clear counters <intf>'
Clear interface counters after fixing to verify errors stop: 'clear counters <intf>'
Clear interface counters after fixing to verify errors stop: 'clear counters <intf>'

root_causes.cause: One side hard-coded to 'speed 1000 duplex full', other side left on 'auto' — the auto side falls back to half duplex
Server NIC configured with forced speed/duplex, switch port on auto
IP phone or legacy device forcing 100/full, switch on auto negotiating 100/half
After replacing hardware, new device defaults to auto while old side retained forced settings

symptoms: Slow throughput on specific links despite gigabit interface speeds
Late collisions, FCS errors, runts incrementing in 'show interface' counters
Link is UP/UP (not down) — makes this hard to detect
Small transfers work fine but large file transfers fail or are extremely slow
Users report intermittent slow network on specific ports

verify_commands.confirms_if: Shows current duplex setting per port
verify_commands.look_for: Check 'Duplex' column — 'a-full' = auto-negotiated full, 'full' = forced full, 'a-half' = auto-negotiated half (potential mismatch)

verify_commands.confirms_if: Shows current duplex setting per port
verify_commands.look_for: Check 'Duplex' column — 'a-full' = auto-negotiated full, 'full' = forced full, 'a-half' = auto-negotiated half (potential mismatch)

verify_commands.confirms_if: Shows current duplex setting per port
verify_commands.look_for: Check 'Duplex' column — 'a-full' = auto-negotiated full, 'full' = forced full, 'a-half' = auto-negotiated half (potential mismatch)

verify_commands.confirms_if: Non-zero late collisions on a full-duplex interface confirms duplex mismatch
verify_commands.look_for: Look for 'late collision', 'FCS errors', 'runts', 'giants' in the error counters — these indicate duplex mismatch

verify_commands.confirms_if: Non-zero late collisions on a full-duplex interface confirms duplex mismatch
verify_commands.look_for: Look for 'late collision', 'FCS errors', 'runts', 'giants' in the error counters — these indicate duplex mismatch

verify_commands.confirms_if: Non-zero late collisions on a full-duplex interface confirms duplex mismatch
verify_commands.look_for: Look for 'late collision', 'FCS errors', 'runts', 'giants' in the error counters — these indicate duplex mismatch

verify_commands.confirms_if: Active errors confirm an ongoing duplex issue
verify_commands.look_for: Look for incrementing Align-Err, FCS-Err, or Late-Col counters

verify_commands.look_for: Look for incrementing Align-Err, FCS-Err, or Late-Col counters

failure_modes.description: The switch CAM table aging timer (default 300s) is much shorter than the router ARP cache timer (default 4 hours on Cisco). The switch ages out a MAC entry while the router still has a valid ARP entry for that IP. Subsequent traffic from the router is forwarded as unknown unicast and flooded to ALL ports in the VLAN, wasting bandwidth and creating a security exposure.

resolutions.action: Align timers: increase MAC aging to match ARP ('mac address-table aging-time 14400') or decrease ARP cache ('arp timeout 300' on router interface)
Align timers: increase MAC aging to match ARP ('mac address-table aging-time 14400') or decrease ARP cache ('arp timeout 300' on router interface)
Align timers: increase MAC aging to match ARP ('mac address-table aging-time 14400') or decrease ARP cache ('arp timeout 300' on router interface)
For silent hosts: configure static MAC entry or use 'ip arp inspection' to generate traffic
For silent hosts: configure static MAC entry or use 'ip arp inspection' to generate traffic
For silent hosts: configure static MAC entry or use 'ip arp inspection' to generate traffic
For asymmetric routing: fix routing to ensure symmetric paths, or configure the MAC statically on both switches
For asymmetric routing: fix routing to ensure symmetric paths, or configure the MAC statically on both switches
For asymmetric routing: fix routing to ensure symmetric paths, or configure the MAC statically on both switches
Monitor: 'show mac address-table count' and 'show interface counters' for unknown unicast flooding indicators
Monitor: 'show mac address-table count' and 'show interface counters' for unknown unicast flooding indicators
Monitor: 'show mac address-table count' and 'show interface counters' for unknown unicast flooding indicators

root_causes.cause: Default CAM aging (300s) vs default ARP aging (14400s/4 hours) creates a 4-hour window where MAC is aged but ARP is valid
Asymmetric routing: traffic ingresses on one switch but egresses on another — the egress switch never learned the destination MAC
Silent hosts: devices that receive traffic but rarely transmit (sensors, cameras) — their MAC ages out because the switch only learns from source MAC
Large L2 domain: CAM table size pressure causes more aggressive aging

failure_modes.scenario: Unicast flooding due to CAM/ARP timer mismatch
symptoms: Unexpectedly high traffic on ports that should not be receiving it
Bandwidth utilization higher than expected on access ports
Packet captures on unrelated ports showing traffic destined for other hosts
'show mac address-table count' shows frequent MAC learning/aging activity
Problem is periodic — occurs after 5 minutes of silence from a host

verify_commands.confirms_if: Shows current MAC aging timer
verify_commands.look_for: Default 300 seconds — compare with ARP cache timeout on the default gateway router

verify_commands.confirms_if: Shows current MAC aging timer
verify_commands.look_for: Default 300 seconds — compare with ARP cache timeout on the default gateway router

verify_commands.confirms_if: Shows current MAC aging timer
verify_commands.look_for: Default 300 seconds — compare with ARP cache timeout on the default gateway router

verify_commands.confirms_if: Shows MAC table utilization and churn rate
verify_commands.look_for: Check total dynamic MACs and free entries — high churn indicates frequent aging/learning

verify_commands.confirms_if: Shows MAC table utilization and churn rate
verify_commands.look_for: Check total dynamic MACs and free entries — high churn indicates frequent aging/learning

verify_commands.look_for: Compare unicast TX counters — if an access port is transmitting far more unicast than expected, it may be receiving flooded traffic

verify_commands.look_for: Compare unicast TX counters — if an access port is transmitting far more unicast than expected, it may be receiving flooded traffic

verify_commands.look_for: Compare unicast TX counters — if an access port is transmitting far more unicast than expected, it may be receiving flooded traffic

failure_modes.description: MTU mismatches in the network cause large packets to be silently dropped. This is especially common with VXLAN (adds 50 bytes overhead) and MPLS (adds 4+ bytes per label) where the underlay MTU must accommodate the overlay encapsulation. Standard ping (64 bytes) works fine, masking the problem, while real application traffic with larger frames fails.

resolutions.action: Test with DF-bit set: 'ping <dest> size 1500 df-bit' (IOS) or 'ping -s 1472 -M do <dest>' (Linux) — timeout indicates MTU problem
Test with DF-bit set: 'ping <dest> size 1500 df-bit' (IOS) or 'ping -s 1472 -M do <dest>' (Linux) — timeout indicates MTU problem
Test with DF-bit set: 'ping <dest> size 1500 df-bit' (IOS) or 'ping -s 1472 -M do <dest>' (Linux) — timeout indicates MTU problem
Check MTU on every hop: 'show interface <intf> | include MTU' on all switches in the path
Check MTU on every hop: 'show interface <intf> | include MTU' on all switches in the path
Check MTU on every hop: 'show interface <intf> | include MTU' on all switches in the path
For VXLAN: set underlay MTU to 9216 on ALL interfaces in the fabric: 'system jumbomtu 9216' (NX-OS) or 'system mtu 9216' (IOS-XE)
For VXLAN: set underlay MTU to 9216 on ALL interfaces in the fabric: 'system jumbomtu 9216' (NX-OS) or 'system mtu 9216' (IOS-XE)
For VXLAN: set underlay MTU to 9216 on ALL interfaces in the fabric: 'system jumbomtu 9216' (NX-OS) or 'system mtu 9216' (IOS-XE)
Ensure ICMP is not blocked: allow ICMP type 3 code 4 (fragmentation needed) through all firewalls and ACLs
Ensure ICMP is not blocked: allow ICMP type 3 code 4 (fragmentation needed) through all firewalls and ACLs
Ensure ICMP is not blocked: allow ICMP type 3 code 4 (fragmentation needed) through all firewalls and ACLs
Verify consistency: all members of a port-channel must have matching MTU
Verify consistency: all members of a port-channel must have matching MTU
Verify consistency: all members of a port-channel must have matching MTU

root_causes.cause: Jumbo frames (MTU 9216) enabled on some switches but not all in the path
VXLAN underlay MTU not increased to accommodate 50-byte VXLAN overhead (need at least 1550, recommend 9216)
ICMP blocked by firewall or ACL — prevents Path MTU Discovery from working
L2 switches do not generate ICMP messages — they silently drop oversized frames without notification
Inconsistent MTU across port-channel members

failure_modes.scenario: MTU mismatch causing silent packet drops
symptoms: Small transfers (ping, DNS, SSH) work fine but large transfers (file copy, SCP, HTTPS downloads) fail or are extremely slow
TCP connections establish but stall during data transfer
Path MTU Discovery fails silently — ICMP 'fragmentation needed' messages blocked or not generated by L2 switches
Applications time out after initial connection succeeds
VXLAN or MPLS overlay traffic fails while underlay pings work

verify_commands.confirms_if: Identifies MTU mismatches between interfaces in the forwarding path
verify_commands.look_for: Check MTU value — compare across all interfaces in the path. Default 1500, jumbo 9216

verify_commands.confirms_if: Identifies MTU mismatches between interfaces in the forwarding path
verify_commands.look_for: Check MTU value — compare across all interfaces in the path. Default 1500, jumbo 9216

verify_commands.look_for: Check MTU value — compare across all interfaces in the path. Default 1500, jumbo 9216

verify_commands.look_for: Check system-level MTU setting — this is the maximum MTU any interface can use

failure_modes.description: Ports placed into errdisable state silently stop forwarding traffic. Multiple features can trigger errdisable: BPDU Guard, port security, UDLD, storm control, link-flap detection, DHCP snooping rate-limit, and others. Without errdisable recovery configured, ports remain down until an administrator manually bounces them — which can take hours if no one notices.

resolutions.action: Identify the cause: 'show errdisable recovery' shows which causes are configured for auto-recovery
Identify the cause: 'show errdisable recovery' shows which causes are configured for auto-recovery
Identify the cause: 'show errdisable recovery' shows which causes are configured for auto-recovery
Check the specific cause: 'show interface status err-disabled' shows affected ports, syslog shows the trigger
Check the specific cause: 'show interface status err-disabled' shows affected ports, syslog shows the trigger
Check the specific cause: 'show interface status err-disabled' shows affected ports, syslog shows the trigger
Fix the root cause before re-enabling: remove the offending device, fix the cable, adjust thresholds
Fix the root cause before re-enabling: remove the offending device, fix the cable, adjust thresholds
Fix the root cause before re-enabling: remove the offending device, fix the cable, adjust thresholds
Re-enable manually: 'shutdown' then 'no shutdown' on the interface
Re-enable manually: 'shutdown' then 'no shutdown' on the interface
Re-enable manually: 'shutdown' then 'no shutdown' on the interface
Enable auto-recovery for common causes: 'errdisable recovery cause bpduguard', 'errdisable recovery cause udld', etc.
Enable auto-recovery for common causes: 'errdisable recovery cause bpduguard', 'errdisable recovery cause udld', etc.
Enable auto-recovery for common causes: 'errdisable recovery cause bpduguard', 'errdisable recovery cause udld', etc.
Set recovery interval: 'errdisable recovery interval 300' (5 minutes)
Set recovery interval: 'errdisable recovery interval 300' (5 minutes)
Set recovery interval: 'errdisable recovery interval 300' (5 minutes)

root_causes.cause: BPDU Guard: switch or hub plugged into an edge/portfast port
Port security: too many MAC addresses learned on a port
UDLD: unidirectional link detected
Storm control: broadcast/multicast threshold exceeded
Link-flap: interface bouncing too rapidly (5 flaps in 10 seconds default)
DHCP snooping: rate limit exceeded on untrusted port
ARP inspection: rate limit exceeded

symptoms: 'show interface status' shows port as 'err-disabled'
Port LED on switch may show amber/orange
Connected device has no network connectivity
Syslog message indicating the cause: '%PM-4-ERR_DISABLE: <cause> error detected on <intf>'
Multiple ports may be affected simultaneously (e.g., link-flap during a storm)

verify_commands.confirms_if: Shows which ports are errdisabled and why
verify_commands.look_for: Lists all ports currently in errdisable state with the reason

verify_commands.look_for: Lists all ports currently in errdisable state with the reason

verify_commands.look_for: Lists all ports currently in errdisable state with the reason

verify_commands.look_for: Check which errdisable causes have auto-recovery enabled and the recovery interval

purpose: Layer 2 Ethernet switching infrastructure — covers VLAN trunking, duplex negotiation, MAC learning, MTU handling, and errdisable mechanisms

timers.description: Time before a dynamically learned MAC address is removed from the CAM table
timers.name: MAC address aging
timers.on_expiry: MAC entry removed from CAM table; subsequent traffic to that MAC is flooded as unknown unicast
timers.tuning_advice: Default 300s is shorter than ARP cache (4 hours on Cisco). This mismatch causes unicast flooding — the router still has ARP but the switch has aged the MAC. Consider increasing to match ARP cache or reducing ARP cache to match.

timers.default_rfc: 300s (if recovery is enabled; disabled by default for most causes)
timers.description: Time before an errdisabled port is automatically re-enabled
timers.name: errdisable recovery interval
timers.tuning_advice: Enable recovery for common causes (bpduguard, udld, link-flap) with 300s interval. Too short = port flaps; too long = extended outage for legitimate devices.

notes.note: IOS-XE system MTU: 'system mtu 9198' (Catalyst 9000) sets the global MTU. Takes effect after reboot on some platforms. Check with 'show system mtu'. For VXLAN, ensure 'system mtu 9198' is configured on all leaf/spine switches.

IOS-XE 'vlan dot1q tag native': tags the native VLAN with 802.1Q header, eliminating native VLAN ambiguity. Recommended security best practice. Both ends of the trunk must have this configured or native VLAN traffic will be dropped.

config_examples:
errdisable recovery cause bpduguard
errdisable recovery cause udld
errdisable recovery cause link-flap-recovery
errdisable recovery interval 300

notes.note: NX-OS system jumbo MTU: 'system jumbomtu 9216' enables jumbo frames globally. Individual interfaces must also have 'mtu 9216' configured. For VXLAN fabrics, jumbo MTU is required on ALL underlay interfaces.

NX-OS errdisable recovery: 'errdisable recovery cause <cause>' enables per-cause auto-recovery. Common causes to enable: bpduguard, udld, link-flap-recovery, storm-control. Set interval with 'errdisable recovery interval 300'. View status: 'show errdisable recovery'.
